new_findings.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

sources.quote: it will sell them a video for $34.99 yes it is slightly cheaper than the $40 I mentioned earlier but this is to warm them up
once they purchase this $34.99 video then you can consider them warmed up and then whenever you sell them a piece of content after that it is going to be at least 40 and upwards
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-side pricing ladder or escalation logic exists (§7 GAP). The external chat team sets its own approach; Palm has no documented position on warm-up pricing or escalation sequencing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a two-phase pricing ladder for the external chat team and future in-house chatters: Phase 1 (new sub warm-up) = first video at ~$35; Phase 2 (warmed fan) = $40+ escalating. Write this into the chat team brief and eventually into Palm's in-house chatter SOP (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

new_findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

new_findings.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
new_findings.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

new_findings.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

new_findings.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

new_findings.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

new_findings.title: Every reel needs hook, retention, reward; 2-3/day across content pillars

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

sources.quote: Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline scores viral reels and decides what to film (§1 MATURE) but does not codify per-reel hook/retention/reward structure or a pillar split-test cadence; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a reel-structure standard (hook/retention/reward) and a pillar-based split-test posting cadence to the inspo pipeline output for real creators; complements Palm's existing scoring engine.

new_findings.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Use a tiered 'script' — escalating PPV ladder from $15 to $200+ per session

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or chat script structure (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). No internal baseline exists for escalation sequencing. This is high-value build-toward intel for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting projects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a canonical escalation script template for chatters: define 5–6 tiers with explicit price points, content-reveal thresholds, and transition copy at each step. Treat it as a reusable asset deployed across all creators. Price anchoring should start low ($15) to minimize entry friction. Prioritize this for the AI chatting project where scripts can be fully automated.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Anchor the PPV ladder at $15 entry point to minimize first-purchase friction

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP — identified as #1 creator concern). This is the clearest external data point available for building Palm's first PPV pricing SOP. The doubling structure is a concrete starting model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this ladder structure as the baseline for Palm's PPV pricing SOP: 6 tiers starting at $15, roughly doubling each step. Document it formally in §7 pricing docs. Test against the external chat team's current pricing to see if a ladder approach could increase per-fan revenue. This also gives Palm a concrete pricing framework to share with creators during onboarding (addressing the #1 creator concern).

new_findings.title: One reusable script deployed across all subscribers = scalable chat ops

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

sources.quote: it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script assets. This 'script as reusable product' model is directly applicable to the AI chatting roadmap project — the state machine should implement the script once and deploy it across all fans on a given creator's account.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI chatting project, build the escalation script as a canonical, versioned asset per creator (not per conversation). Track which 'step' in the script each fan has reached using Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so the AI resumes at the correct point. For the in-house chat team, train chatters on script delivery as a skill (making canned feel personal) rather than improv.

new_findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

new_findings.title: On-shift group call + peer-authority system accelerates collective chatter improvement

variants.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
variants.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.

sources.quote: having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat team structure at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team. Relevant also to AI chatting project's team design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up the in-house chat team, create a Telegram or Discord group call as an 'on-shift room.' Map each chatter's strongest skill in Airtable and use that routing table to build peer mentorship from week one.

new_findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
new_findings.title: Build a professional IG persona to run cold outreach to creators

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

new_findings.title: Google Form onboarding captures creator profile, device specs, goals, and personal traits

variants.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.

sources.quote: get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard (name, email, commission %, state, contract, voice memo — §6) but no documented device requirement, content style intake, or goal-setting fields. The iPhone 13+ quality gate is not in Palm's onboarding.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard to include: (1) device model field with a minimum spec gate (iPhone 13+ or Android equivalent), (2) content style/persona fields, (3) creator goals (revenue target, posting comfort level, explicit limits). Add an OF profile audit step as a formal onboarding milestone before first post.

new_findings.title: Build a linear pre-made script ladder: message → soft image → message → explicit PPV

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).

sources.quote: you want to make one message, then another, then maybe send her a half nude image, then another message, then a full nude, then blah blah blah
80 to 90% is made through chatting — the models, they're busy creating content, so they don't actually have time to do all the chatting

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team today (§8). No internal script, ladder structure, or SOP exists. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team (~1yr out) and the active AI chatting project. Frame as build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin drafting Palm's first pre-made script ladder now as a build-toward asset: (1) map a 5–7 step message sequence per creator persona, (2) define PPV price points per step (ties to §7 pricing gap), (3) document it in Airtable as a 'Chat Scripts' table linked to each creator. This becomes the training baseline when in-house chatting launches.

new_findings.title: Train chatters with agency-owned PPV scripts rather than relying on their defaults

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

sources.quote: you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced with no internal scripts or SOPs (§8). When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), it will need proprietary script libraries and training material. This competitor model — agency owns the playbook, chatters execute — is the architecture Palm should adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin building Palm's internal PPV script library now, drawing from competitor frameworks like this one. Structure it as: (1) welcome/intro message template, (2) conversation bait posts, (3) upsell ladder scripts per price tier, (4) objection-handling / repackaging scripts. This becomes the training corpus for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.

new_findings.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

new_findings.topic: Commonality building as the core sales engine
new_findings.title: Three-tier commonality system (me-too / mutual contact / tangential connection) to manufacture emotional bond

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Notably, the 'plausible reason for validation' logic is essentially applied sales psychology that Palm could encode as a training principle, not just a chatting script.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make Level 3 commonality building the centrepiece of Palm's in-house chatter training. Create a worked-example library organized by profession/hobby (e.g., 'if sub says he's a nurse, here are three Level 3 connections'). Pair this with the qualification CRM data so chatters can prep connections before re-engaging a returning sub.

new_findings.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

new_findings.title: Use Guaranteed Gains to buy a fixed subscriber count from larger pages

variants.claim: Paying a larger creator or agency a flat fee to guarantee a specific number of new free fans is a cost-effective cold-traffic channel, especially for trans pages.
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a page owner a fixed dollar amount (example: ~$140 for 500 GGs or 1,000 GGs) to post the new creator's page plus send a mass DM to their fan list, guaranteeing at least that many new free subscribers. ROI is tracked as: fans acquired × LTV minus cost. Example given: 200 GGs at $500 cost, $11 LTV = $2,200 revenue, $1,700 profit. Deals are sourced through OFM Telegram groups and tracked in a spreadsheet. A dedicated 'queue manager' role schedules and verifies every promo fires on time, creates tracking links, and runs profit/loss analysis per promo to identify which partners to scale.

sources.quote: I will give you 200 of my fans guaranteed for 500 bucks
queue managers are creating tracking links and running profit analysis on every single promo

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic or paid-distribution strategy (§5 GAP). No equivalent of guaranteed-gains deal-sourcing, queue-manager role, or per-promo ROI tracking exists in the Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight GG tracking sheet (partner, cost, fans guaranteed, fans delivered, LTV estimate, profit) even before activating this channel. For real creators in fetish-adjacent niches, test one GG deal with a Telegram-sourced partner at a small budget ($200–$500) to validate LTV assumptions. Appoint or assign queue-manager responsibility to an existing ops role so timing and compliance don't slip.

new_findings.title: Build a Telegram-sourced S4S network before signing creators to pre-load launch traffic

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

sources.quote: you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented S4S or network-building playbook (§5 GAP). The system is entirely absent from Palm's current ops baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Assign a part-time 'network getter' task (could be Josh or a VA) to join 5–10 OFM Telegram groups and begin logging trans-creator and agency contacts into an Airtable table (Name, Platform, Fan Count, Contact Method, Last Outreach, Deal Type). Even if Palm doesn't operate trans creators today, the mechanics of S4S and launch-day pre-scheduling are transferable to any niche and should be piloted on the next real-creator launch.

new_findings.title: Separate 'network getter' and 'queue manager' roles to operationalize promotion at scale

variants.claim: A $100K/month promo machine requires two distinct ops roles: one that expands the contact network daily and one that schedules, tracks, and enforces timing on every active promotion deal.
variants.what_they_do: Network getters are in Telegram groups every day adding new creator and agency contacts. Queue managers hold the schedule of every live S4S and GG deal, confirm partners post on time, create unique tracking links per promo, and run profit analysis to identify high-ROI partners for reinvestment. A separate media buying team handles paid IG meme-page buys. Reddit/X contractors distribute content as an additional traffic stream. All roles are concurrent and ongoing, not launch-only.

sources.quote: your network getters are reaching out to new leads and creators every day expanding the size of your network
your queue managers are constantly in combo with all of your network arranging shout out for shout out and GGs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Amin for manual posting and an external chat team but no equivalent network-getter or queue-manager role (§10 THIN). Traffic strategy is an explicit GAP (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to activate a distribution playbook, define these two roles explicitly in the org chart before hiring. In the short term, create a simple Airtable table ('Promo Queue') with fields: Partner, Deal Type, Cost, Scheduled Date, Confirmed Posted (Y/N), Tracking Link, Fans Delivered, Profit. This infrastructure costs nothing to build now and makes the queue-manager role immediately hirable when needed.

new_findings.topic: Reddit and X as supplementary traffic channels
new_findings.title: Contract out Reddit and X content distribution as a low-overhead traffic stream

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit or X traffic strategy (§5 GAP). This is a pure learn-from-others gap that applies to real creators and is relatively easy to test at low cost.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source a Reddit/X contractor via OFM Telegram groups. Start with a 30-day paid pilot (~$500–$1K) for one creator, using UTM-tagged links or OF referral tracking to attribute revenue. If 5x+ ROI is confirmed, formalize as a recurring line item per creator.

new_findings.title: Gate paid cold-traffic spend on known LTV; only scale if margins are confirmed

variants.claim: The decision to run VA-led cold outreach or any paid cold-traffic campaign should be gated on whether the creator's LTV is high enough to make the acquisition cost profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Before activating VA cold-outreach or media buying, the agency calculates the estimated LTV per free fan (example used: $11/fan) and sets a maximum allowable acquisition cost per fan based on a target margin (example: willing to pay up to $5,000 for 500 GGs = $10/fan, expecting $11 LTV). Every promo deal is evaluated against this threshold before committing budget. Tactics that don't meet the margin threshold are not activated regardless of how low-cost they appear.

sources.quote: cold traffic lead generation with VAs outreaching to cold audience and converting them — if your LTV is high enough, this will make sense
I am going to be willing to pay upwards of $5,000 for 500 GGs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented LTV per fan figure, no PPV pricing model, and no acquisition-cost threshold (§7 GAP, §6 GAP). Without these numbers, Palm cannot evaluate any paid traffic channel rationally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As a prerequisite to any paid traffic or GG experimentation, calculate LTV per free fan for at least one existing creator using historical OF data (total revenue ÷ total free fans over a rolling 90-day window). Store this in Airtable per creator. Even a rough number unlocks rational spend decisions across all traffic channels.

new_findings.topic: Pre-launch network building before signing creators
new_findings.title: Build distribution network of 10–20 partner creators before signing your first creator in a niche

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

sources.quote: before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led and reactive (§6 THIN). There is no documented pre-signing network-building step or launch-day traffic playbook. Palm typically figures out distribution after a creator is onboarded.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any new niche Palm enters (including trans if that becomes a focus), add a pre-launch checklist step: minimum N partner contacts secured in Telegram before creator onboarding begins. Document required contacts and deal commitments as a gate in the creator onboarding flow, making distribution readiness a precondition of launch rather than an afterthought.

new_findings.title: Choose fetish niches (e.g. trans) over mainstream niches to unlock paid traffic channels and reduce parasocial dependency

variants.claim: Trans and fetish-adjacent niches are structurally easier to scale to $100K/month because fans are content-driven rather than parasocial-relationship-driven, making cold-traffic channels like GGs and media buying viable without a warm-up funnel.
variants.what_they_do: Agency deliberately targets fetish-driven niches where the fan–creator relationship is transactional. In these niches, cold traffic from meme pages, GGs, and S4S converts directly to paying subscribers without needing a Cupid-style warm-up sequence required for mainstream (cis) creators. This structural difference means the traffic stack is simpler, cheaper, and more scalable. The operator cites knowing multiple agencies doing million-dollar profit months exclusively via trans agencies.

sources.quote: I know a couple guys in the OFM space that are doing million-dollar months — they're doing it with a trans agency
in the trans community, people that are into this type of stuff don't care about the creator — they just want to get their hands on as much as they possibly can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's niche strategy is not documented (§6 GAP, §7 GAP). Palm currently uses Cupid-style warming for cis creators (referenced in transcript as the workaround for cold traffic not converting). The structural point — that fetish niches eliminate the warm-up dependency — is a strategic insight Palm should weigh when evaluating creator acquisition targets.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm evaluates new creator signings, add a niche-type field to the creator intake process (fetish vs. mainstream) and document the expected traffic stack per type. If Palm experiments with trans or other fetish creators, expect that GG and S4S channels will convert more efficiently and that the warm-up content requirement (§1 pipeline) can be reduced or replaced with a faster paid-traffic playbook.

new_findings.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

new_findings.title: Remove all visible price menus from the OF bio — menus make the page feel like an eBay store

variants.claim: Displaying a price menu in the OF bio immediately cheapens the creator's brand and signals transactionality before any relationship is built.
variants.what_they_do: When taking over a creator account, the first thing this agency removes is the bio price menu (e.g. '$5 photos, $10 packs'). No prices are listed publicly. Pricing is handled exclusively inside DM conversations after rapport is established, which allows for dynamic escalation and avoids anchoring fans to low numbers.

sources.quote: the first thing I see when I take over a models only fans account are all these offers something like this there'll be a menu in the bio
as soon as guys subscribe to the only fans account they see this and it feels like an eBay store

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented OF page setup or bio standards (§7 GAP, §2 THIN). No internal policy on whether price menus should or should not appear in the bio.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a Palm account setup standard: no price menus in the OF bio. Add this to the creator onboarding checklist and to the brief given to the external chat team when taking over an account.

new_findings.title: Predictable creator-signing via paid ad funnel

variants.claim: A productized paid-ads-to-VSL-to-call funnel turns creator acquisition from referral/DM luck into a predictable, scalable pipeline.
variants.what_they_do: Runs paid ads (or organic traffic) to a lead magnet, then a 5-minute direct VSL whose two jobs are to book qualified calls and pre-handle objections; a solid funnel + sales team books 20-40 calls/month at a 75-85% show rate and pre-closes 30-40%.

sources.quote: how we built a funnel that books qualified creator calls on autopilot and pre-closes about 30 to 40% of them
you can quite easily be booking 20 to 40 calls per month, and have a show rate of 75 to 85%

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound + referrals with no systematic machine (§6 THIN). No funnel, lead magnet, or VSL exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a creator-facing funnel: one lead magnet + a short VSL, runnable on organic traffic first (no ad spend), to convert Josh's outbound and any inbound into booked, pre-qualified calls.

new_findings.title: Delegate menial work to protect creative output

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

sources.quote: you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already delegates manual posting to a contractor (Amin, §5) and editing to editors (§1), but has no formalized delegation/hiring plan (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a delegation ladder (what the founder keeps vs hands off) and hiring criteria, building on Palm's existing posting/editing outsourcing.

new_findings.title: Track the full outreach funnel and fix one stage at a time

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach funnel metrics (reply/booking/show/close) or follow-up cadence (§6 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Stand up an outbound tracker (reply, qualified, booked, showed, closed) and a multi-touch follow-up sequence, mirroring the data-driven discipline Palm already applies to content.

new_findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

new_findings.title: Charge no upfront or hidden fees; earn only on a partnership split

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm produces a contract PDF at onboarding but has no documented fee-structure stance (§7 GAP); this is a concrete trust-positioning rule to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 'no upfront, no hidden fees, partner-not-asset' as explicit Palm acquisition trust positioning.

new_findings.topic: systemize then scale one creator at a time
new_findings.title: Systemize day-one onboarding and perfect one creator before scaling

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is multi-creator past this stage, but the 'systemize before you scale' discipline maps onto Palm's THIN/GAP areas (acquisition §6, pricing §7, retention §9) that aren't yet documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply 'document and systemize before delegating/scaling' to close Palm's THIN areas before further growth.

new_findings.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

palm_comparison.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

new_findings.title: Anchor on a 50% full-service split, don't undersell at 20-30%

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale/tiers (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's commission rationale with a 50% default anchor and explicit guidance on when to deviate, so splits aren't negotiated ad hoc.

new_findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

new_findings.title: Build content around the creator's niche and her own strengths

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is data-driven and mature for ideation (§1), but niche/positioning and creator-strength fit are not explicit fields in the flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a niche + creator-strengths step feeding the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas are filtered for fit, not just virality.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Onboard with a documented same-day process and a welcome video

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding is a form wizard → Creators table (§6); strong on contract automation but no documented expectation-setting welcome video or same-day activation SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a standardized welcome video + same-day activation checklist to onboarding to set expectations and cut early churn.

new_findings.title: Steer on CAC:LTV and margin, not followers

variants.claim: Allocating resources by cost-per-acquisition versus LTV and target margin, rather than by follower counts or a raw LTV number, is how a scaled agency decides where to invest.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media tracks free and paid LTV over 7 and 30 days against a roughly $1 Reddit CAC, targets a 50% margin on base traffic (accepting 20-30% on low-effort purchased traffic), and color-codes models so investment flows to the highest CAC:LTV returns; the founder calls 'what is a good LTV' the wrong question.

sources.quote: we are always monitoring free LTV and paid LTV over a 7-day and 30-day period
what is a good LTV... it's just the wrong question... what we actually care about is cac to ltv

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data but leadership reporting is in-flight and there is no standard CAC:LTV or margin-per-creator view (§9 PARTIAL, §7 THIN). Gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a per-creator CAC:LTV dashboard with a target margin, and allocate posting/acquisition spend by that ratio instead of by follower vanity metrics.

new_findings.title: Use the fan's name, kill mass-messages

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but personalization data and tone live with outsourced chatters (§8). Build-toward plus owning the data.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Ensure Palm's own per-fan CRM captures the name and interests, and bar generic mass-message bundle blasts, so personalization survives any chatting-vendor change and feeds future AI chat.

new_findings.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

new_findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
new_findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

new_findings.topic: Multi-account content rotation — avoiding identical Reels grids
new_findings.title: Rotate which repurposed video goes to which account — never mirror the same grid

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

sources.quote: if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current scheduling system (§5) routes posts via Publer or Telegram/Amin but is designed for one primary account per creator. There is no documented logic for distributing content across a cluster of repurposing accounts for a single creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a distribution-rotation rule in the scheduling layer: for any creator running N repurposing accounts, assign each daily video to a unique subset of those accounts via a round-robin or modular assignment in Airtable/Publer. This prevents grid mirroring and reduces ban risk.

new_findings.topic: Organic vs. repurposed account architecture — strict separation
new_findings.title: Never mix organic and repurposed content on the same IG account

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

sources.quote: you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) is entirely organic/creator-filmed for the primary account. Palm does not currently run a repurposing-account layer. This finding clarifies the correct architecture IF Palm adds one: keep the existing organic pipeline untouched and stand up a separate repurposing operation alongside it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm scales to multi-account reposting, codify the two-tier rule in the SMM hub redesign (§11): tag accounts as 'organic-primary' or 'repurpose-only' in Airtable, and enforce via posting-workflow routing so editors never schedule repurposed content to an organic-primary account.

new_findings.title: Start organic on Twitter, TikTok, IG only — add Reddit/bots later

variants.claim: New agencies should limit traffic sources to organic short-form content on Twitter, TikTok, and Instagram, deferring Reddit, bots, and shoutouts until the funnel is proven.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends a three-platform organic-only traffic strategy for beginners: push short-form video content on Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram. Explicitly deprioritizes Reddit, paid shoutouts, and automation tools as advanced tactics — not because they don't work, but because complexity at the start obscures what's actually driving results.

sources.quote: start simple things like Twitter, Tik Tok, Instagram, and that's it. Just push organic short form content. No need to over complicate it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG and social content production (§1) but has no written playbook for multi-platform distribution sequencing or when to layer in Reddit/paid channels.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For new creator onboarding, define a Phase 1 traffic stack (IG + TikTok organic, consistent with Palm's existing content pipeline) and a Phase 2 expansion trigger (e.g., once a creator hits X subs or Y MRR, add Reddit and X/Twitter). Document this as a traffic phase ladder so operators know when and how to escalate.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

new_findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

new_findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

new_findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

new_findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.
