sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

new_findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

new_findings.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

new_findings.title: Run weekly payouts and performance check-ins as a retention and motivation tool

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm auto-computes weekly invoices (§7) so the cadence matches. However, Palm has no documented creator-facing performance review or check-in tied to the payout — it's finance-only. No retention/success cadence is written down (§9 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Attach a lightweight weekly creator report to Palm's existing invoice email: top 3 metrics (subscribers, revenue, top-performing post). Takes minimal effort given Palm's Airtable data and Resend pipeline, and transforms a finance touchpoint into a retention and upsell moment.

new_findings.title: Batch shoot 5 looks → batch schedule 5 days of page posts + paired PPV messages

variants.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
variants.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.

sources.quote: say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is strong on ideation and editing but posting cadence for real creators is not codified (§1 🔲 GAP). The OF vault/PPV side (§2) has no batch-scheduling SOP. This tactic bridges both gaps with a concrete daily-output model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a batch content session standard for Palm real creators: minimum 3 looks per session, each look producing 2–3 photos (page) + 1 video (PPV). Ops team schedules page posts and paired PPV messages together in one Publer/OF scheduling pass. Add a 'batch session' record type to the Airtable Content Ops base to track sessions, looks, scheduled dates, and PPV prices per asset.

new_findings.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
new_findings.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

new_findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

new_findings.title: Cross-platform username matching: find OF creators on X, then locate on IG

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented prospect-sourcing method beyond Josh's personal DMs and referrals (§6). No cross-platform lookup or IG graph-traversal tactic is codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a prospect-sourcing SOP using both methods: (1) X keyword search → IG username lookup, and (2) IG related-accounts traversal from any known OF creator. This is low-cost and immediately actionable for whoever runs outreach at Palm.

new_findings.title: First DM: short curiosity-piquing message focused on a specific observable problem

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

sources.quote: focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message framework or script (§6 GAP). First-contact approach is entirely at Josh's discretion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a first-DM template library: short (1–3 lines), profile-specific observation + curiosity hook, no agency pitch. Test from a personal or agency IG account. This is a direct complement to Palm's existing pipeline — once a creator is signed the content engine (§1) kicks in immediately.

new_findings.title: Google Drive onboarding folder: info sheet + logins + pre-made content scripts

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding collects name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo via a form wizard (§6), but has no documented model info sheet for chatters, no platform-login collection protocol, and no pre-made content script intake. Communication boundary-setting is not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add three items to Palm's onboarding wizard or a linked Google Drive template: (1) a model info sheet with personal details for the chatting team, (2) a platform-login collection step, (3) a pre-made content upload prompt. Also add a written communication expectations section (channel, hours) to the onboarding flow to reduce creator churn friction.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator a hobby-based niche to give the algorithm a clear target audience

variants.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
variants.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.

sources.quote: if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on data-driven ideation from viral inspo reels but does not document a niche-assignment step per creator. The system scrapes what is already viral broadly; it does not codify the step of choosing one thematic niche per creator to anchor the algorithm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'niche selection' step to Palm's creator onboarding and content pipeline (§1, step 5 or pre-step). For each creator, define one or two content niches based on their genuine interests. Feed that niche as a filter into the Apify scrape / Inspo Sources targeting so viral reference reels are pulled from that niche's top performers, not just broadly popular content.

new_findings.title: Escalating PPV price ladder: free photo → $15 → $50 → $100 → $150 → $200

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

sources.quote: the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP, scripts, or pricing ladder documented. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team roadmap and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this escalating PPV ladder as the foundational script template for Palm's future in-house chat team. Specifically: define free-teaser → low-anchor PPV → mid-tier PPV → high-tier PPV price points; have creators pre-shoot content sequences in order before chatters launch; use South Africa as primary chatter sourcing market for English-speaking creator accounts. Brief the external chat team on whether they already use this structure.

new_findings.title: Do chatting yourself for 2 weeks before hiring, then hire South African chatters for cultural fluency

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

sources.quote: step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting entirely today (§8); this tactic is build-toward for the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap). No internal SOP exists for chatter hiring geography or shift structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, use this framework: (1) have the team lead personally chat for 2 weeks per creator to develop training materials, (2) prioritize South African candidates for English-language creator accounts, (3) structure 3-chatter rotating 8-hour shifts per active creator account. Document this in the chat-team hiring SOP before recruitment begins.

new_findings.title: Position the agency offer around creator outcomes (time saved, revenue growth), not the 50% split

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or offer framing (§7 GAP). Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding with no stated rationale or tiering. This is Palm's #1 identified gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's standard offer narrative: lead with the two pain points (fan growth, time), show a concrete growth trajectory from an existing client, and frame the 50% split as a performance-aligned partnership rather than a fee. Consider creating a one-page 'offer doc' with revenue-growth evidence that can be shared during acquisition outreach.

new_findings.title: Never sell PPV packs or bundles — sell individual pieces only

variants.claim: Selling content in packs commoditizes it and undermines the per-item price anchoring that drives high revenue per fan.
variants.what_they_do: The agency explicitly bans 'packs' as a pricing format. All PPV is sold as individual items at or above the price floors. The reasoning is that packs reframe the value proposition toward volume/discount, which is the same psychological trap as low per-item pricing.

sources.quote: you especially don't want to sell packs no packs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented stance on packs vs. individual PPV pricing (§7 GAP). No internal policy exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no packs' rule to the Palm pricing policy alongside the price floors. Instruct the external chat team accordingly. When building the in-house chat SOP, codify individual-item-only PPV selling as a default.

new_findings.title: Require upfront tips before producing any custom content to prevent rip-offs

variants.claim: Fans will request custom content and then disappear after delivery, so requiring tips before production is non-negotiable.
variants.what_they_do: For custom content ($300+), the agency requires the fan to tip multiple times before the creator starts filming. The rationale is to assume all fans will attempt to get content without paying, so payment must be front-loaded. No tip = no custom.

sources.quote: they're going to tip you multiple times before you even start cuz otherwise you're going to make it and they're just going to rip you off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm policy on custom content fulfillment order exists (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). The external chat team handles this ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a customs fulfillment rule to the chat team brief: minimum $300 for customs, all payment (or at least a meaningful deposit tip) collected before any production begins. Document this in the future in-house chatter SOP.

new_findings.title: Deploy an automated new-subscriber script that creates a scenario and sells a first PPV at ~$35

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). No automated new-subscriber script is documented on the Palm side. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI chatting project and in-house chat roadmap (§8): design a standardized new-subscriber automation that immediately engages the fan, creates a light scenario, and attempts a ~$35 first PPV sale. This de-risks the cold-fan problem and reduces reliance on live chatter time for conversion.

new_findings.topic: CRM for fan spend tracking to guide PPV pricing
new_findings.title: Use a fan CRM (e.g. Inflow) to read spending habits and calibrate per-fan PPV price escalation

variants.claim: A CRM that surfaces each fan's historical spend enables chatters to know exactly how far to push pricing on any individual fan rather than guessing.
variants.what_they_do: The agency uses a CRM tool called 'Inflow' that tracks per-subscriber spending history. Chatters consult this before sending a PPV to decide at what price point to pitch — fans who have spent more get higher asks. The principle is always to move price upward based on observed willingness to pay.

sources.quote: if you're using a CRM a customer relationship manager like inflow then those things are going to show you the spending habits for Subs spending habits and then based on these spending habits it shows then you can only go upwards from there

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting tool and Fan Tracker CRM (§9, PARTIAL) that tracks fan data. However, Palm's CRM is primarily a leadership/reporting layer; there is no documented policy for chatters to use fan spend data to calibrate real-time PPV pricing. The external chat team likely does not have structured access to Palm's fan intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bridge the §9 fan intelligence system to the external chat team: share per-fan spend summaries so chatters can price escalate based on data rather than guesswork. For the in-house chat roadmap, build a spend-aware pricing prompt directly into the chatter workflow.

new_findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Confirms Palm's core OnlyFans funnel as the high-conversion asset (§0). Palm's AI personas use OnlyFans-via-social, so this validates Palm's choice not to go Fanvue-only.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold the OnlyFans funnel as the conversion anchor for both real and AI lines; treat Fanvue-style platforms only as fallback, never primary.

new_findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

new_findings.title: Engagement-trap, trendjack, and paid promo pages on X

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

sources.quote: the content designed to get replies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is undocumented (§5 gap). These are specific paid + organic X tactics with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture as build-out menu for an X traffic SOP: engagement-trap caption library, daily trend monitoring, and a tested promo-page budget per creator.

new_findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

new_findings.title: Bans driven by unbranded sexual content, not IP/proxies

variants.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
variants.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.

sources.quote: 90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is hardware-isolated with proxies/device separation (§3/§4); for REAL creators Palm has no documented IG ban-avoidance posture (§4 gap). This reframes the real-creator risk as content-led.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For real-creator IG, prioritize branded non-explicit content and normal-user behavior over technical spoofing; document a real-creator ban-avoidance SOP.

new_findings.title: Branded persona replacing pure volume in 2026

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline optimizes for viral content ideation (§1) but persona/branding is not a codified input. This argues brand should be a first-class field upstream of content selection.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a defined persona/brand brief to creator onboarding and feed it into the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas reinforce a consistent identity.

new_findings.title: Snapchat as an under-saturated OF traffic + income channel

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is IG/TikTok via Publer + Telegram (§5); Snapchat is absent from Palm's stack. New channel with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot Snapchat for one high-output creator: repurpose existing content, weekly-only CTA, test promoted snaps; measure as a third revenue lever.

new_findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

new_findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

new_findings.title: Ladder PPV prices upward in sequence

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: No formal price ladder at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Publish a standard Palm PPV ladder with rising steps and trigger conditions, instead of single high asks.
